text: This is a case of a fifty-six-year-old male who had a Lichtenstein repair for an indirect inguinal hernia a few years ago. The patient has a questionable recurrence, but also a de novo direct inguinal hernia, as you can see.
The surgeon begins dissection laterally in Bogros' space, progressing medially towards the supravesicular space, creating a peritoneal flap. The peritoneal flap appears thicker in older patients.

The surgeon dissects past the medial umbilical ligament, extending to the contralateral medial umbilical ligament, and continues into the supravesicular space. The dissection crosses into the peritoneal flap, with the surgeon working laterally to push fat and tissue laterally, aiming to cover nerves along the lateral side wall.

The surgical field shows a standard direct inguinal hernia with no visible evidence of recurrence in the direct space. The indirect space near the deep internal ring appears slightly adhesive, but no mesh is visible.

Again, I kind of course a little bit more medially to the contralateral medial umbilical ligament, especially in directs. I like to have plenty of space and, you know, doing my Ten Commandments going past the symphysis pubis.
The surgeon uses an oblique camera angle to visualize the antisac, gently pushing it away while occasionally applying cautery to facilitate tissue separation.
The surgeon maintains dissection along Cooper's ligament and the symphysis pubis, ensuring precise adherence to these anatomical landmarks.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field, ensuring the obturator plug is removed and checking for an obturator hernia. The visual examination confirms the absence of a direct hernia, recurrent hernia sac, and cord lipoma.

The surgeon completes the dissection steps, maintaining focus on anatomical landmarks such as Cooper's ligament and the symphysis pubis, while ensuring no evidence of recurrent hernia or cord lipoma is present.

The surgeon positions the mesh laterally to ensure it lays flat and does not roll when closing the flap. Two-ovicral sutures are used to fixate the mesh to Cooper's ligament, with high lateral and medial fixation near the direct hernia sac.
The surgeon performs lateral fixation of the mesh, ensuring it is not overly tightened to avoid wrinkling, while positioning it medial to Cooper's ligament and just medial to the iliac vein.
The surgeon carefully positions the mesh close to the iliac vein, ensuring it is near the symphysis pubis in the middle to prevent recurrence or tissue protrusion.

The surgeon achieves triangular fixation of the direct inguinal hernia space, focusing on the high medial portion. The fixation is visible on the right side, while the stitch on the bottom left is not visible. The surgeon cinches the fixation to keep the mesh flat, followed by a standard peritoneal flap closure using two OV lock sutures.
